fr:mainmatter.html:p: The hardware optimization on GPUs that combines simultaneous memory accesses by threads in a Warp scheduling into a single transaction — when the accessed addresses fall in the same cache line.
Global memory latency on a GPU is 400–800 cycles. Coalescing is the primary way to keep the SM fed:

html:ul.html:li: If all 32 threads in a warp access addresses within one cache line: one memory transaction, full bandwidth.
If addresses span multiple cache lines: the hardware serializes into multiple transactions; bandwidth drops proportionally.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Address permutations within a cache line don't hurt utilization — coalescing is about which lines are touched, not which exact bytes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Coalescing is why memory layout matters so much for GPU code. The standard idiom is structure-of-arrays (SoA) instead of array-of-structures (AoS): threads accessing the same field of consecutive records hit consecutive bytes, which coalesce; threads accessing the same struct don't.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Should you always cache on the GPU?

No. If you're memory-bound and have no Spatial locality, cache is wasted on data you won't reuse. CUDA exposes the option to disable L1 caching for these cases, which lets misaligned loads transfer only the bytes needed instead of full cache lines.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Concept-level notes on multicore architecture, the cache-coherence stack, memory consistency, on-chip networks, and data-level parallelism. Mostly distilled from an EECS course; cross-references into Notebook: Inside the Machine (Inside the Machine) for single-core/uniprocessor topics.
The observation that as transistor dimensions shrink by a factor \alpha , you can also drop voltage by \alpha , keeping power per transistor constant:
In the Dennard era, every process shrink got more transistors at the same power budget — the foundation of decades of frequency scaling.
Post-Dennard. Below ~65nm, voltage can no longer be scaled because the supply-to-threshold ratio V_{dd}/V_{th} must stay above ~3 for transistors to switch reliably. The consequences:

html:ul.html:li: Power per transistor scales only as 1/\alpha , not 1/\alpha ^2.
Power density therefore scales as \alpha — denser chips dissipate more per unit area.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is the same constraint that drove the Power wall in commercial CPUs: single-core frequency scaling stopped working, and the industry pivoted to multicore and energy-efficient architectures.

An alternative to fine-grained Locks: mark code regions as transactions that should appear atomic, execute them speculatively in parallel, and detect conflicts after the fact. Aims at the performance of fine-grained locking with the programming ergonomics of coarse-grained locking.
Two transactions conflict if they access the same memory location and at least one of them is a write. The implementation tracks per-transaction read and write sets.

html:ul.html:li: Lazy detection. Build the entire read/write set as the transaction runs; check for conflicts only at commit time. If conflict, roll back.
Eager detection. Check on every access against other transactions' sets; abort immediately on conflict.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Eager detection can piggyback on the cache coherence protocol: add read/write bits to each cache line, set them during a transaction, and let a coherence invalidation signal a conflict.
Two operations: commit and abort. Implementations:

html:ol.html:li: Lazy: stores go to a "write table" rather than memory; loads check the table first, falling back to memory. Commit writes the table back (slow); abort just clears the table (fast).

Eager (coherence-backed): stores write through but track read/write sets per cache line. Commit clears the sets (fast); abort clears the sets (also fast — work already on overwrite if not aborted before commit).

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: If a transaction touches more cache lines than the cache holds, the tracking overflows.
Fall back to software transactional memory (STM) on overflow — a hybrid scheme that keeps hardware fast-path for common cases.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A lock is a synchronization primitive that grants mutually exclusive access to a critical section. The implementations below trade off coherence traffic, fairness, and memory in different ways. The naive locks (Test-and-set lock and Test-and-test-and-set lock) are simple but generate O(n) coherence traffic per release; queue-based locks (Ticket lock, Array-based lock, MCS lock) scale better at the cost of extra state per waiter.

The simplest spin lock. The acquire loop spins on an atomic test_and_set: it writes LOCKED and reads the previous value in one step. If the previous value was UNLOCKED, the caller has the lock; otherwise it tries again.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Major coherence traffic. Every spinning core must acquire write permissions and invalidate everyone else on each test_and_set, so contention scales badly with the number of waiters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A refinement of the Test-and-set lock that reduces coherence traffic during the spin. Threads first perform a plain load to check whether the lock looks free, and only attempt the atomic test_and_set when it does. Spinning is then read-only, so cores can share the cache line.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Spinning is a read-only operation now.

When the lock is released, the line must be invalidated on every waiter, and they all simultaneously read and try to write. The contention is shifted from steady state to release time but is still O(n) in the number of waiters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A fair spin lock based on two counters. Each arriving thread takes a "ticket" by atomically incrementing next_ticket and then spins until now_serving equals its ticket. Release simply increments now_serving. Threads acquire the lock in FIFO order, which prevents starvation but does not by itself reduce contention.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
acquire(lock_ptr): my_ticket = fetch_and_increment(lock_ptr->next_ticket) while (lock_ptr->now_serving != my_ticket); // spin
release(lock_ptr): lock_ptr->now_serving = lock_ptr->now_serving + 1

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Still O(n) coherence traffic on release: incrementing now_serving invalidates the line on every waiter, and they all reload it to recheck their ticket.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each waiter is assigned its own slot in an array and spins only on that slot. Release just flips the next slot to "go-ahead", so the cache line touched by release is the one read by exactly one other thread. Coherence traffic per release is constant rather than O(n), at the cost of one slot per waiter.

html:ul.html:li: Give each waiter its own "slot" to wait on.
Only need to set the next slot to "go-ahead" on release.
Constant coherence traffic.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: O(n) memory for the lock array, and padding each slot to 64b (one cache line) to avoid false sharing makes that cost real.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A scalable, list-based queue lock. The lock variable is a pointer to the tail of a linked list of per-thread nodes. Each thread enqueues its own node and spins on a flag in its own cache line. The releaser wakes only its direct successor, so coherence traffic per handoff is constant and memory cost is proportional only to threads currently waiting, not to the lock's fanout.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
acquire(lock): I->next = null predecessor = fetch_and_store(lock, I) if predecessor != nil: // some node holds the lock I->must_wait = true predecessor->next = I // predecessor must wake us repeat while I->must_wait // spin till lock is free
release(lock): if (I->next == null): // no known successor if compare_and_swap(lock, I, nil): // make sure... return // CAS succeeded; lock freed repeat while I->next == nil // spin to learn successor I->next->must_wait = false // wake successor

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In a Multicore system where every core has its own Cache hierarchy, the same memory address can live in multiple caches simultaneously. A cache coherence protocol is the mechanism that keeps those copies consistent — propagating writes, invalidating stale copies, and preserving two invariants:

html:ol.html:li: Single-writer invariant: at any moment, at most one core has permission to write a given line. No other core can read or write it concurrently.
Data-value invariant: all cores see the same order of updates to a given line.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two organizational families:

html:ul.html:li: Snoop-based coherence — every cache watches a shared bus and reacts to other cores' traffic. Scales to ~8 cores.
Directory-based coherence — a structure tracks who has a copy of each line; coherence messages are point-to-point. Scales to hundreds of cores at the cost of an extra hop.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The concrete state machines are protocols like MSI protocol, MESI protocol, MOESI protocol, and DEC Firefly, each adding states to handle more cases or to optimize specific sharing patterns. A worked implementation of directory-based MESI in Murphi lives in Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi.
Worked instance: Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi implements directory-based MESI in Murphi.

The strongest practical memory model. The result of any execution is the same as if the operations of all threads were executed in some single sequential order, and the operations of each thread appear in that order in the same sequence as the program specifies.

Equivalent operational definition: imagine a single switch that, at each step, selects one thread to run its next operation. Any valid execution is the result of some sequence of such selections. There is no reordering across threads, no reordering within a thread, and no buffering that becomes visible to other threads.

Sequential consistency is what most programmers reason about by default — and what real hardware does not provide, because it forbids almost every optimization that makes a CPU fast. Relaxed consistency is what we get in exchange.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: SC does not provide guarantees between threads beyond ordering. Two threads racing on the same variable are still racing — SC just promises a consistent global timeline.

Enforcing SC purely in hardware requires loads to wait for the store buffer to drain and stores to retire strictly in order. Both kill performance. SC-preserving compilers try to push the constraint to compile time by refusing to reorder shared accesses.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A memory model weaker than Sequential consistency that allows the hardware (and compiler) to reorder memory operations for performance, with explicit fence instructions where the programmer wants to forbid reordering.
A fence is implemented with a counter — incremented on issue of each memory op, decremented on completion. The fence stalls dispatch until the counter reaches zero.

html:ul.html:li: TSO (Total Store Order) — loads may be hoisted above prior stores. The motivation: a store can sit in the store buffer waiting for cache, while subsequent loads (which don't touch the buffered address) proceed. x86 implements TSO.

RMO (Relaxed Memory Order) — anything may be reordered. Stronger reorderings still need to be justified per case:
Load → store: no performance gain to reorder; leave alone.
Store → store: keep ordering with a slightly larger store buffer.
Load → load: speculate the second load while waiting for the first; reorder is invisible if the speculation is verified.
IBM 370 variant — TSO + no store forwarding (preserves global store atomicity).

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Under TSO, Dekker's algorithm is still broken without fences — the classic example where TSO is too weak for naive software synchronization.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In RMO you still need fences to ensure data-race freedom. Release consistency relaxes the fences themselves:

html:ol.html:li: Full fence — no instruction may cross in either direction.

Half fence (acquire/release):
Acquire — operations after may not move above.
Release — operations before may not move below. Expanding a critical section is fine; entering it (acquire) must not let subsequent ops sneak above; exiting (release) must not let prior ops sneak below.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: C++ std::atomic compiles to half-fences in the right places, which is why it is the standard primitive for portable lock-free code.

Two accesses to the same memory location, from different threads, where at least one is a write and there is no happens-before relationship between them. The happens-before relation is what synchronization primitives like Locks and atomics induce — when one access happens before another, they are not racing.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Competing for the same lock is not a data race. Whichever thread acquires first happens before the next; the access inside each critical section is sequentially ordered.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Data-race freedom (DRF) is the implicit contract behind every relaxed memory model: if your program has no data races, Relaxed consistency hardware behaves as if it were Sequential consistency. Conversely, in the presence of a data race, the result is undefined by the language and the model.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Writing data-race-free programs is hard. Treating every shared access as a synchronization point destroys performance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: SC-preserving compilers approach: forbid the compiler from reordering shared accesses, leaving the hardware free to do TSO-style reordering. The optimization most disrupted is common-subexpression elimination across shared accesses — solved by inserting a "monitor" check on the coherence bit between expressions.

Hardware approach. Treat all pages as local/safe by default and extend page protection bits to the thread level: a non-owning thread accessing the page triggers a fault, exposing the data race at runtime.

Single instruction, multiple data. Extend the Fetch-execute loop so that one fetch+decode applies the same operation to a vector of operands. The fetch-decode overhead amortizes over the vector width; loop bookkeeping (increment, compare, branch) is replaced by a single vector op.
Concretely, a vector ISA adds:

html:ul.html:li: A vector register file — wider than scalar registers; specify the data width per element and total bits per register.
Vector load/store instructions for moving contiguous chunks of memory to/from vector registers efficiently.
Vector predication (masking) — apply the op only where the mask is true, allowing data-dependent control flow without scalar branches.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Trade-offs:

html:ul.html:li: Need the array length to be divisible by the vector width for perfect speedup; tail handling is annoying.
Vector pipelines cannot do Forwarding cheaply — would need O(n^2) connections to every ALU.
Wider hardware vectors can be supported on narrower hardware by microcoded decomposition; backward compatibility falls out for free.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In practice, vector code is generated by:

html:ol.html:li: Intrinsic functions (architecture-specific C builtins).
Vector data types in a language extension.
External libraries (BLAS, etc.).
Compiler auto-vectorization.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Aliasing: modifying a vector you're also reading from causes a race within the operation.
SIMD is hardware-driven data parallelism. The GPU descendant is SIMT, which softens many of SIMD's rigidities.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A GPU sacrifices single-thread latency for aggregate throughput. The opposite design point from a CPU:
The GPU equivalent of a core is a streaming multiprocessor (SM). Each SM executes the same instruction across many threads in lockstep — but with a key difference from Vector ISA / SIMD:
Single instruction, multiple threads. The SM issues one instruction per cycle to a group of threads (a Warp scheduling), but each thread keeps its own program counter and register state.
Advantages over SIMD:

html:ol.html:li: Control-flow divergence. Threads on different branches of a conditional execute dummy instructions on the inactive path until reconvergence. The compiler / hardware mask the inactive lanes; the programmer doesn't have to vectorize manually.
Grouping flexibility. Hardware (or the compiler) can re-group threads into warps based on actual control flow — see "warp specialization".

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: How do GPUs deal with cache misses given they don't have OoO machinery?
Fast context-switching between warps. When a warp stalls on memory, the SM switches to another runnable warp with already-resident state. Enough warps means the SM is always busy.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In a CPU, context switching is expensive — registers must be saved to memory. On a GPU, each SM has an enormous register file that holds the registers of all resident warps simultaneously, so a context switch is just changing which warp the scheduler picks.

html:ol.html:li: Kernel — the launched function.
Blocks — groups of threads sharing fast on-chip shared memory (Shared memory and bank conflicts).
Warps — 32 threads issued together in lockstep (Warp scheduling).

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The physical size of shared memory constrains the number of resident threads per SM.
Use GPUs for parallel parts where throughput dominates. Use CPUs for sequential parts where latency dominates.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A warp is 32 threads on an NVIDIA GPU executing in lockstep — one instruction issued per cycle to all 32. The SM's warp scheduler picks which resident warp to run on each cycle.

The point of the scheduler: hide stalls. When a warp issues a memory operation that misses cache, it can't run for hundreds of cycles. With many resident warps, the scheduler always has a runnable one. Aggregate throughput stays high even though individual-warp latency is awful.

Occupancy is the ratio of active warps to maximum allowed warps per SM. Higher occupancy means more candidates for the scheduler to switch to, which means more latency hiding. Low occupancy means the SM stalls because every resident warp is waiting on the same memory transaction.
For latency-bound workloads (and most GPU code is), maximizing occupancy is the first-order optimization.

When threads in a warp take different branches of a conditional, the SM must serialize: execute one branch with its threads active and the others masked off, then the other. The lanes on the inactive path execute dummy instructions, wasting their compute slots.
Mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Structure code so threads in a warp follow the same control flow when possible.
Warp specialization: have the compiler group threads with the same control-flow pattern into the same warp.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The hardware optimization on GPUs that combines simultaneous memory accesses by threads in a Warp scheduling into a single transaction — when the accessed addresses fall in the same cache line.
Global memory latency on a GPU is 400–800 cycles. Coalescing is the primary way to keep the SM fed:

html:ul.html:li: If all 32 threads in a warp access addresses within one cache line: one memory transaction, full bandwidth.
If addresses span multiple cache lines: the hardware serializes into multiple transactions; bandwidth drops proportionally.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Address permutations within a cache line don't hurt utilization — coalescing is about which lines are touched, not which exact bytes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Coalescing is why memory layout matters so much for GPU code. The standard idiom is structure-of-arrays (SoA) instead of array-of-structures (AoS): threads accessing the same field of consecutive records hit consecutive bytes, which coalesce; threads accessing the same struct don't.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Should you always cache on the GPU?

No. If you're memory-bound and have no Spatial locality, cache is wasted on data you won't reuse. CUDA exposes the option to disable L1 caching for these cases, which lets misaligned loads transfer only the bytes needed instead of full cache lines.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Shared memory on a GPU is a small (per-block) on-chip scratchpad with latency comparable to L1. Software-managed — the programmer explicitly stages data through it, the hardware does not cache for them. Used to:

html:ul.html:li: Avoid non-coalesced global accesses by reorganizing data layouts on the fly.
Reuse loaded data across threads within a block.
Communicate between threads within a block.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Shared memory is divided into banks (typically 32). Each bank can service one access per cycle. If two threads in a Warp scheduling access different addresses in the same bank, the accesses serialize — a bank conflict.
Three behaviors when warp threads access shared memory:

html:ul.html:li: Different banks: full parallelism. No conflict.
Same bank, same word: hardware broadcasts the word to all threads. No conflict.
Same bank, different words: serialize.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: How to avoid bank conflicts?
Change the access pattern so threads hit different banks (often by transposing or by adding padding to break the stride that causes the conflict).

The GPU memory hierarchy is software-managed at L1 (shared memory) and coherent only at L2 — by restricting L1 to local-to-block scope, the architecture sidesteps the cost of a global Cache coherence protocol on a thousand-way-parallel chip.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Application-Specific Integrated Circuits fuse multiple operations into hardware that a general-purpose pipeline would have to schedule sequentially. The motivating intuition: if you frequently multiply-accumulate, the multiply and the add each traverse the entire pipeline separately. A dedicated multiply-accumulate ALU does both in one cycle.
The cost-of-operation gap is large — 32-bit vs 8-bit addition has roughly an order of magnitude difference in energy per op. ASICs win by matching width to need.

fr:title.text: Notebook: Computer Architecture › Processing in memory

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Computer Architecture › Processing in memory

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Notes on guest lecture by Yufeng Gu titled Architecture and System Co-Design for Scalable Large Language Model Inference
TFLOPs increasing much faster than DRAM bandwidth -> Hardware scaling is hitting the memory wall.
Data center power consumption also increasing \approx 10% a year.

html:p.html:strong: GPUs are under low utilization for LLM inference

html:ul.html:li: Prefix stage: parallel
Decoding stage: sequential -> bottleneck!

fr:mainmatter.html:p: As batch size increases, the KV-cache dominates memory usage.

html:ul.html:li: KV cache size grows with context length.
Tradeoff between context length and batch size.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Unlike fully-connected cannot batch self-attention because it depends on the individual prompts.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Recall: operational intensity is \frac {\text {compute throughput}}{\text {memory bandwidth}}

html:p.html:strong: Inference much lower operational intensity than GEMM!

html:ul.html:li: Inference only doing matrix \times row
If embedding dim is D, then this is only D ops / byte.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Processing in memory (PIM) enables faster memory bandwidth.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Has lower memory capacity (fewer GB) due to added processing units (PU).
Use Compute eXpress Link (CXL) to do memory expansion to get high memory bandwidth + expend memory capacity.

Put high use operations i.e. vector multiplication, element-wise multiplication in PIM module (99% usage). Put more complicated operations on PNM accelerator. TODO: ISAs for each specified on slides (not available yet).
CENT supports inter and intra-device communication via connecting communication controller to shared buffer and CXL port.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Communcation primitives: multicast and gather

html:ul.html:li: Multicast: send to all CXL devices.
Gather: receive data sequentially from all CXL devices.
Multicsat-gather -> tensor parallel.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Peer-to-peer: can also support pipeline parallelism.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: For fast communication, use tensor parallelism i.e. if connected via NVLink. If slower communication use pipeline parallelism. Can mix.
Longer context -> better performance for PIM (TODO: why?). HBM + PIM does not scale. Cannot arbitrarily add PIM.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The network connecting cores, L2/L3 banks, and memory controllers on a Multicore chip. The same abstractions that govern off-chip networks (topology, routing, flow control) apply, but the constraints are different: wires are abundant but die area is scarce, latency is dominated by hops rather than propagation, and the network is part of the Cache coherence critical path.
The measurements used to compare network topologies and traffic patterns.

html:ul.html:li: Degree: number of links at each node. A proxy for router complexity — each port costs a buffer, crossbar input, state machine.

Bisection bandwidth: bandwidth across a cut that splits the network into two equal-sized halves. The minimum over all such cuts. A proxy for peak achievable throughput, especially for uniformly random traffic.
Diameter: maximum hop distance between any two nodes. The worst-case hop count.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Bisection bandwidth is less useful for on-chip networks than off-chip because global on-chip wiring is plentiful.
Ring bisection is 2 (two cuts to bisect); torus bisection is 4.

html:ul.html:li: Hop count: hops between two specific nodes. The average over a traffic distribution is a useful proxy for latency.
Maximum channel load: the most-loaded link's load relative to per-node injection bandwidth, for a given traffic pattern. The proxy for sustained throughput.
Path diversity: number of distinct shortest paths between two nodes. Higher path diversity allows adaptive routing to spread load.
Zero-load latency: average hop count with no contention.
Saturation throughput: the injection rate at which latency curves upward exponentially.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Hop count is not the same as latency. Per-hop pipeline depth and link propagation matter — more short hops can beat fewer slow ones. Router pipeline depth isn't always known in advance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Two organizational families for on-chip networks.

html:ul.html:li: Direct — every terminal node (core, memory controller) is associated with a router. Most on-chip designs are direct because area is precious and there's no point spending a router with no compute attached. The "k-ary n-cube" family (Ring topology, Mesh topology, Torus topology) is direct.

Indirect — routers may exist that connect only to other routers, with no terminal attached. Butterfly network and Clos network are indirect; a router in their middle stages just shuffles bits between input and output stages.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Indirect networks can shorten the diameter for the same node count, but they pay for extra silicon area for routers that don't serve traffic locally. Often the right call off-chip; usually the wrong call on-chip.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The simplest indirect network: an n \times m grid of switch points connecting n inputs to m outputs. Any input can be connected to any free output simultaneously — non-blocking.

html:ul.html:li: One hop per transit (minimum possible).
Serializes if multiple senders target the same receiver.
Switch area grows as O(n \cdot m) — the dominant cost as systems grow.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Crossbar router area scales as the square of the port count, which is why high-radix routers are dramatically more expensive than low-radix ones.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Crossbars are common as the inner switch of larger structured networks (Clos network, Fat tree) — large flat crossbars are impractical at scale, but small crossbars are cheap.

A direct network where each node connects to two neighbors, forming a closed loop. Bidirectional rings allow either direction of travel; unidirectional rings allow only one. The simplest topology to implement and reason about.
In the k-ary n-cube family, a ring is a k-ary 1-cube.

html:ul.html:li: Degree 2 (every node).
Diameter \lfloor k/2 \rfloor for a bidirectional k-node ring.
Bisection bandwidth 2 — cut the ring at exactly two links.
Hardware overhead very low: routers have two ports.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Worst-case hop count is O(k). Doesn't scale beyond a small number of nodes.
Despite the poor scaling, rings are common in commercial CPUs (Intel's L3 ring bus on Sandy/Ivy Bridge) because they're cheap and 8–16 cores is enough.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A 2D grid of nodes with neighbors connected on the four compass directions, no wrap-around. The dominant on-chip topology in commercial designs (Xeon Phi, recent server x86 chips) because it lays out naturally on a 2D die.

html:ul.html:li: Interior nodes degree 4; edge nodes degree 3; corners degree 2.
Average hop count for an even-k mesh: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{3}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/4 — twice that of a Torus topology.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Non-uniform node degree. Edge nodes carry less traffic than they could, while interior nodes bottleneck on traffic flowing through them.

Mesh dominates Torus topology in practice despite the topology metrics being worse, because torus wrap-around wires are physically long and force expensive folding to equalize link lengths. Manufacturing wins over theoretical performance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Mesh topology with wrap-around links connecting the ends of each dimension. In the k-ary n-cube classification, a k-ary n-cube. Uniform node degree (every node has degree 2n), uniform load distribution, half the hop count of a mesh.

html:ul.html:li: Every node has degree 2n.
Average hop count for even k: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{4}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/8 — half that of a Mesh topology.
Good path diversity: many shortest paths between any two nodes.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Wrap-around links are physically long. To equalize wire delays, the torus must be folded, doubling effective wire lengths and link area. The wrap-around also doubles the link count versus a mesh of the same dimensions.
Despite the theoretical advantage, mesh wins on real chips. Tori show up in HPC interconnects (Cray) where the wires can run on dedicated cables.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Variants:

html:ul.html:li: k-ary n-cube: k nodes along each of n dimensions, all ends wrapped.
Asymmetric: (i, j, k)-ary — different node count per dimension.
Mesh: same as torus but without wrap-around.
Ring: k-ary 1-cube.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A multistage indirect network. A k-ary n-fly has k^n terminal nodes connected through n stages of k \times k crossbar switches. At each stage, the routing decision selects which of k sub-groups to enter; after n stages, every terminal can reach every other.

html:ul.html:li: Hop count n - 1.
Under uniform random traffic, maximum channel load is 1 (perfectly balanced).
Channel load drops further for non-uniform traffic that respects the natural partitioning.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Zero path diversity. There is exactly one path between any source and destination. A single hot link wedges traffic.
Add shuffle stages that permute paths after each routing stage, so the same source-destination pair can be reached by multiple routes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A three-stage indirect network parameterized by (m, n, r): r input switches and r output switches each of size n \times m, with m middle-stage switches each r \times r. Total terminal nodes N = nr. Non-blocking when m \geq 2n - 1.

html:ul.html:li: Number of total switching elements scales as N^{1.5} versus N^2 for a flat Crossbar while preserving non-blocking.
Path diversity is m — multiple distinct paths between any source and destination.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: There are rmn input-switch elements, mr^2 middle-switch elements, and rmn output-switch elements: S = 2rmn + mr^2. With r = N/n: S = 2Nm + m(N/n)^2. Choosing n = \sqrt {N} and m \geq 2n - 1 = O(\sqrt {N}): S = 2mN + 2m\sqrt {N} = O(N^{1.5}).
A folded Clos network — input and output stages combined, making n input ports and m output ports bidirectional — is structurally the same as a Fat tree.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A tree topology with bandwidth that doubles at every level toward the root. Each interior switch has logical degree 4, but as you climb the tree, link width doubles to absorb the combined traffic of its subtree — preserving bisection bandwidth.

Structurally, a fat tree is a folded Clos network: the input and output stages of a Clos are merged into a single bidirectional stage at each level of the tree, and the middle stage becomes the higher levels of the tree.

html:ul.html:li: Each switch has logical degree 4 (2 down, 2 up — with link doubling).
Bisection bandwidth scales linearly with the number of leaves.
Path diversity grows toward the root.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Fat trees are the dominant topology for off-chip HPC interconnects (InfiniBand fat-tree clusters). Less common on-chip because the doubling links are hard to lay out on a 2D die.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given a topology and a packet's source and destination, which path through the network does it take? Three classes of answer:

html:ul.html:li:
Greedy (shortest-path) — always pick the shortest available route.
(+) Minimizes latency under light load.
(-) Overloads central links; worst-case throughput poor.

Uniform / oblivious random — pick a direction at random.
(+) Spreads load; avoids systematic hotspots.
(-) Wastes bandwidth on non-minimal paths; latency unpredictable.

Adaptive — at each hop, pick the outgoing link with the lowest local congestion.
(+) Dynamically balances load; best worst-case throughput.
(-) Routers need congestion state; risk of livelock without care.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Tornado traffic on an 8-node bidirectional ring: node i sends to (i+3) \bmod 8.
Greedy: all packets travel counter-clockwise. Bidirectional bandwidth wasted; \frac {1}{3} utilization.
Uniform: randomly clockwise/counter-clockwise. Counter-clockwise gets \frac {2}{3} utilization, clockwise \frac {2}{5}; worst-case \frac {2}{5}.
Adaptive: sends \frac {5}{8} counter-clockwise to match traffic exactly. Best.
Adaptive routing tends to need Network backpressure signaling to know where congestion is, and care to avoid livelock when many adaptive decisions chase each other.

Oblivious strategies like Dimension-order routing and Valiant's routing algorithm give deterministic guarantees (deadlock-free, bounded path length) at the cost of giving up the load-balancing of true adaptive routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An oblivious Network routing strategies for k-ary n-cube networks (Mesh topology, Torus topology): always exhaust the offset in one dimension completely before changing to the next. E.g. in 2D mesh routing, route X-then-Y — never turn back to X after starting Y.

The dimension order prevents routing deadlock: cycles in the channel dependency graph are impossible because no packet ever requests a channel in a "lower" dimension once it has started using a higher one. The dependency graph is therefore acyclic, and the standard cycle-equals-deadlock argument applies.

html:ul.html:li: (+) Provably deadlock-free without virtual channels.
(-) Zero path diversity. Hot spots can't be routed around.
(-) Unaware of current network state — oblivious.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The standard remedy for the path-diversity problem is Valiant's routing algorithm, which keeps the deadlock-freedom of dimension-order routing while spreading load with a randomized intermediate hop.
An oblivious Network routing strategies that achieves provably optimal worst-case throughput by introducing randomness in path selection:

html:ol.html:li: For a packet going from s to d, randomly pick an intermediate node w.
Route s \to w using Dimension-order routing.
Eject the packet at w, then re-inject it.
Route w \to d using Dimension-order routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To preserve deadlock-freedom, the intermediate node must remove the packet from the network before re-injecting — otherwise the channel dependencies of the two halves could interlock.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Restricting w to lie in the sub-quadrant defined by s and d keeps paths near-minimal while still spreading load — the practical compromise between guaranteed throughput and added latency.

Valiant's algorithm trades average-case latency (paths are longer) for worst-case throughput (any traffic pattern looks roughly uniform once randomized). The right call for adversarial workloads; overkill for uniform traffic.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Routing deadlock occurs when a packet cannot make progress because the buffer at its next link is full, and the packet holding that buffer is itself blocked waiting on a buffer this packet holds — a cycle in the channel dependency graph.
Two structural responses:

html:ol.html:li: Avoid deadlock by construction. Dimension-order routing enforces an ordering on dimension transitions so the dependency graph is acyclic by design.

Detect and recover from deadlock. Allow all turns. Detect cycles via timeouts. Drain stuck packets onto a backup network (a "slow channel" — physical, or more commonly an escape virtual channel) that uses dimension-order routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A virtual channel (VC) is a separate logical buffer that shares the same physical link with other VCs. Multiple VCs on one link let the network distinguish classes of traffic — if one VC fills, others on the same link still flow.
Two motivations for VCs:

html:ul.html:li: Routing-deadlock avoidance: provide an escape channel routed via DOR.

Protocol-deadlock avoidance: separate message classes (request, response, forwarded) onto distinct VCs so a stuck response cannot block a request that would have unstuck it. See Implementing Directory-based MESI in Murphi for a worked Murphi example where 3 VCs are required for the directory protocol.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The relationship to other related concepts:

html:ul.html:li: Backpressure (Network backpressure) is the signaling mechanism that lets a router downstream tell upstream it is congested, before the upstream actually fills the link.
Adaptive routing avoids deadlock locally; backpressure lets adaptive routing react to remote congestion.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A signaling mechanism that lets a downstream router tell upstream "I am congested — slow down" before its buffers actually overflow. With backpressure information, adaptive routing can route around remote congestion, not just local.

The mechanism propagates: a congested link signals back to its sender, which then knows its next link will fill, which signals further back. Useful information flows in the opposite direction of the data.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Acting only on local state can cause livelock — packets bounce between hotspots, never converging.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Backpressure breaks the livelock by giving local routers global-ish information without an actual global view.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Backpressure is mechanism; adaptive routing is policy. The two together are what real high-radix on-chip networks use.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The on-chip network connects each core (with its L1) and the L2 cache banks (which may be shared or private). Each L2 bank has a cache controller (manages Cache coherence) and, at memory boundaries, a memory controller (talks to DRAM).
Two cache-organization choices interact with the network design:

html:ul.html:li: Private L2 caches: each tile owns its L2 slice; cross-tile traffic happens only on private-L2 miss. (+) Lower hit latency; less interconnect traffic. (-) Replicated data; more off-chip traffic on misses.
Shared L2 cache: a single logical L2 split into banks, with each address mapped to a home bank. (+) Better utilization, no replication. (-) Every L1 miss goes on the network.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The interface to the network sits at the cache controller:

html:ul.html:li: Miss status handling register (MSHR): on a cache miss, records what request the cache is waiting on. Matched against the reply when it arrives.
Transaction status handling register (TSHR): tracks outstanding memory transactions to off-chip memory from the directory cache.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Memory controllers are commonly placed at the chip perimeter, near the I/O pads. A memory controller can share a router with a core/cache tile (saves area; needs arbitration) or have its own (more area; no contention).
Hardware-managed shared memory is one design point; another is explicit message passing, where software (not hardware) is responsible for moving data between processes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Links. Thicker wires with larger spacing → lower resistance, higher speed, better energy efficiency. The wire budget determines how many ports you can afford per router.

Routers. Each port costs a buffer, a crossbar input, state logic, and a link to its neighbor. Crossbar area scales as the square of port count, so high-radix routers are dramatically expensive. Rings have very simple routers but poor performance.

Floorplan. Tori need to be folded to equalize wire lengths, doubling effective wire length; meshes don't. Tori also have twice as many links as meshes, which may force narrower links and lower per-link bandwidth.
Folding can also equalize path lengths inside a network at the cost of increasing average latency.
Concentration allocates more cores per router. (-) Decreases throughput per node. (+) Programs with high locality do little remote communication, so concentration is a good fit there.
General topologies aren't always optimal. Two construction strategies:

html:ul.html:li: Splitting: start with a large crossbar; split into smaller switches until constraints are met.
Merging: start with many small switches and combine.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Hierarchical topologies use one topology within clusters and another across clusters — common when traffic has locality.
A warp is 32 threads on an NVIDIA GPU executing in lockstep — one instruction issued per cycle to all 32. The SM's warp scheduler picks which resident warp to run on each cycle.

The point of the scheduler: hide stalls. When a warp issues a memory operation that misses cache, it can't run for hundreds of cycles. With many resident warps, the scheduler always has a runnable one. Aggregate throughput stays high even though individual-warp latency is awful.

Occupancy is the ratio of active warps to maximum allowed warps per SM. Higher occupancy means more candidates for the scheduler to switch to, which means more latency hiding. Low occupancy means the SM stalls because every resident warp is waiting on the same memory transaction.
For latency-bound workloads (and most GPU code is), maximizing occupancy is the first-order optimization.

When threads in a warp take different branches of a conditional, the SM must serialize: execute one branch with its threads active and the others masked off, then the other. The lanes on the inactive path execute dummy instructions, wasting their compute slots.
Mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Structure code so threads in a warp follow the same control flow when possible.
Warp specialization: have the compiler group threads with the same control-flow pattern into the same warp.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The empirical observation that if a program accesses memory address x, it is likely to access addresses near x soon. Sequential array traversal, struct field access, and instruction fetch all exhibit it.

Caches exploit spatial locality by loading whole cache lines (typically 64 bytes) on every miss, not just the single requested byte. The byte that triggered the load is the critical word; the surrounding bytes come along for free under the assumption you'll want them.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: RAM and caches are organized in cache-line granularity throughout. Bandwidth is sized for whole-line transfers; tags are per-line, not per-byte.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The complement is Temporal locality — repeated access to the same data over time. Temporal locality implies spatial locality (you re-touch nearby bytes when you re-touch the original), but not vice versa.
